name: Kintaro
description: Kintaro, also known as the Golden Boy, is a prominent figure in Japanese folklore, often depicted as a child prodigy possessing superhuman strength and courage. He is not strictly a deity in the Shinto pantheon, but rather a legendary hero who is frequently celebrated in art, literature, and festivals, particularly during Children's Day. Kintaro's origins are shrouded in mystery, with varying accounts of his parentage and upbringing. He is typically portrayed as a robust and cheerful child, clad only in a diamond-patterned bib (haragake), wielding an axe and befriending animals in the mountains. His story embodies themes of strength, bravery, and filial piety, making him a beloved symbol of ideal childhood virtues and a figure often invoked for protection and good fortune, especially for young boys. Kintaro's legend is intertwined with the tales of Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raiko), to whom he eventually becomes a loyal retainer, showcasing his transition from a wild child to a valued member of society.
subtitle: The Golden Boy

keyMyths.title: Kintaro and the Bear
keyMyths.description: One of the most famous stories of Kintaro depicts him wrestling a bear cub. This tale highlights his extraordinary strength and his affinity with animals. He befriends the bear, and together they roam the mountains, demonstrating his exceptional ability to connect with nature. The bear wrestling scene is a common motif in depictions of Kintaro, symbolizing his untamed power and his harmonious relationship with the wilderness. This myth establishes Kintaro's early prowess and his connection to the natural world.

keyMyths.title: Kintaro and the Koi Fish
keyMyths.description: Another popular myth tells of Kintaro capturing a giant Koi fish. This feat showcases his incredible strength and determination. The Koi fish, a symbol of perseverance and good fortune in Japanese culture, represents a significant challenge overcome by Kintaro. This story reinforces his image as a powerful and resourceful individual, capable of achieving extraordinary feats. The Koi is often depicted as being larger than Kintaro, further emphasizing his remarkable strength.
keyMyths.source: Hearn, Lafcadio. *Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things*. Boston: Little, Brown, 1904.

keyMyths.title: Kintaro Becomes a Retainer of Minamoto no Yorimitsu
keyMyths.description: As Kintaro grows older, his legendary strength and bravery attract the attention of Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raiko), a renowned warrior. Yorimitsu recruits Kintaro as one of his retainers, renaming him Sakata no Kintoki. This transition marks Kintaro's integration into society and his contribution to the military prowess of Yorimitsu's band. This myth highlights Kintaro's loyalty and his transformation from a wild child to a respected warrior, demonstrating his adaptability and his value to the community.
keyMyths.source: De Visser, Marinus Willem. *The Dragon in China and Japan*. Amsterdam: Johannes Müller, 1913.

sources.author: Hearn, Lafcadio
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things

sources.work: The Dragon in China and Japan
sources.description: A comprehensive study of dragons in East Asian culture, with references to Kintaro's association with strength and power.
